calendar.matters:
MOTION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE
Off Calendar Jan-14-2014 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: ROBERT WYMAN, ESQ., 415.387.8107, FOR PETITIONER VERONICA MARIONA.
REBECCA LIU, DEPUTY CITY ATTORNEY, 415.554.3870, FOR RESPONDENT RETIREMENT BOARD OF THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO.

RULING: Argued and the Court adopted its tentative ruling: PETITIONER VERONICA MARIONA's MOTION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE is Granted. Upon the Court's independent review of the Administrative Record, the weight of the evidence does not support the Administrative Law Judge's finding that Petitioner was not disabled for the performance of her duties at the time of her retirement. (See CCP 1094.5) Petitioner has met her burden of showing that she was incapacitated because of her disability, as she was substantially unable to perform the usual duties of her Certified Nursing Assistant position. (See Administrative Record 32-50, 117-134, 136-139, 140-145; see also Mansperger v. Public Employees' Retirement System (1970) 6 Cal.App.3d 873.) Dr. Jacob was clear that Petitioner could not go back to work. Both Dr. Lipton and Dr. Missirian qualified their opinions by stating that Petitioner could go back to work subject to restrictions on her ability to perform various duties. The only evidence concerning "usual duties" came from Petitioner's testimony, and the various restrictions on lifting, bending, pushing and so forth fundamentally affect those usual duties. This point is confirmed by the September 23, 2011 letter from the employer stating that it did not have available work within her work restrictions when she applied for medically appropriate work. (Administrative Record 53.) Although Dr. Missirian suggested a belief that Petitioner might someday regain full range of motion, that appears to be based on some doubts about the course of her treatment and her commitment to it, issues as to which Dr. Missirian had no information-as reflected in his "wonder[ing] if she had appropriate" care. Petitioner's physical therapy records are not in the Administrative Record nor is there evidence that Petitioner failed to participate fully in prescribed physical therapy. As such, there is no evidence that Petitioner refused or failed to undergo reasonable treatment that would have allowed her to return to work. (See Reynolds v. City of San Carlos (1981) 126 Cal.App.3d 208.) Order signed in open court. Judge: Peter J. Busch, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Reporter: Tony Vaughn, CSR #6185
